intent1: Build a multi-service deployment pipeline orchestrator as a CLI tool at `/app/pipeline.sh`.

rounds.title: Build a multi-service deployment pipeline orchestrator as a CLI tool at `/app/pipeline.sh`
rounds.md:
Build a multi-service deployment pipeline orchestrator as a CLI tool at `/app/pipeline.sh`.

The system manages end-to-end build, test, and deploy workflows for a microservices architecture. The project already has a YAML manifest at `/app/pipeline.yaml` and a set of service directories under `/app/services/`. The manifest defines the services, their dependency relationships, and the target environments.

The pipeline orchestrator needs to support the following commands:

**`pipeline.sh validate`** — Parse and validate the manifest. Check for YAML syntax, required service fields (path, build_cmd, test_cmd, port, depends_on, health_endpoint), required environment fields (deploy_target, health_check_port, max_replicas), no duplicate port assignments, no circular dependencies, and all `depends_on` references point to defined services. Print `VALID` on success, or `ERROR: <description>` with the first error found.

**`pipeline.sh order`** — Print the topologically-sorted build/deploy order, one service per line, respecting `depends_on`. When multiple services could go next (same topological level), sort them alphabetically.

**`pipeline.sh build [--service NAME] [--parallel]`** — Simulate building services. Without `--service`, build all in dependency order. With `--service NAME`, build only that service plus its transitive dependencies. Each successful build creates a build artifact at `<service_path>/build/artifact.txt` containing `<service_name>:<epoch_seconds>:built`. Print `BUILD <service_name> OK` per service, or `BUILD <service_name> FAIL: <reason>` if the service path doesn't exist.

With `--parallel`, instead of building, output parallelizable build waves — each line lists comma-separated services (alphabetically sorted within each wave) that can be built concurrently. Services in the same wave have no dependencies on each other within the set being built. When both `--service` and `--parallel` are specified, output waves restricted to the named service and its transitive dependencies.

**`pipeline.sh test [--service NAME] [--fail-fast]`** — Simulate running tests. Without `--service`, test all services in dependency order. With `--service`, test only the named service. A test passes if the service's `build/artifact.txt` exists. Print `TEST <service_name> PASS` or `TEST <service_name> FAIL: not built`. With `--fail-fast`, stop at the first failure. The test command records results internally so that `deploy` can later verify which services have passing tests.

**`pipeline.sh deploy <environment> [--service NAME] [--dry-run]`** — Deploy services to a target environment. Validates the environment exists, then for each service checks that it has a recorded passing test result (from the most recent `test` run for that service). Deploys in dependency order. With `--service NAME`, deploy only that service plus its transitive dependencies. With `--dry-run`, print `DRY-RUN deploy to <env>:` followed by `  Would deploy <service>` lines without actually deploying. On successful deploy, print `DEPLOY <service_name> -> <environment> OK`. If a service's tests haven't passed, print `DEPLOY <service_name> -> <environment> FAIL: tests not passing`. Deployment state must persist so that `status` and `rollback` commands work.

**`pipeline.sh status [<environment>]`** — Without an environment argument, print one line per environment: `<environment>: <count> services deployed`. With an environment argument, print per-service detail sorted alphabetically: `<service_name>: <version> (deployed at <ISO8601_UTC_timestamp>)` where version is the full content of the service's build artifact (e.g., `api-gateway:1713500000:built`).

**`pipeline.sh rollback <environment> <service_name>`** — Remove a service's deployment from an environment. Rollback operates purely on recorded deployment state, not on the manifest — if no deployment record exists for the given environment (whether or not it appears in the manifest), the service is considered not deployed. Print `ROLLBACK <service_name> from <environment> OK` on success. Print `ROLLBACK <service_name> from <environment> FAIL: not deployed` if the service is not currently deployed in that environment.

**`pipeline.sh graph [--format dot|text]`** — Visualize the dependency graph. `--format text` (default) shows a tree rooted at services with no dependencies. Each root's children are the services that directly list it in their `depends_on`. The tree is expanded depth-first, children sorted alphabetically at each level, with 2-space indentation per depth level. A service that appears under multiple parents is shown at each occurrence (full tree expansion of the DAG). `--format dot` outputs Graphviz DOT format with edges from dependency to dependent.

For the sample manifest, `graph --format text` output would be:
```
api-gateway
  order-service
    analytics-service
    notification-service
  user-service
    analytics-service
    order-service
      analytics-service
      notification-service
```

All error cases must output a message starting with `ERROR:`. The tool should manage its own internal state however it sees fit — the observable contract is the stdout output of each command plus the build artifacts.

rounds.title: Extend the pipeline orchestrator with three new commands that build on the existing deploy
rounds.md:
Extend the pipeline orchestrator with three new commands that build on the existing deployment infrastructure: `promote`, `diff`, and `history`. Additionally, all deployment-related commands must now record events into a per-environment event log so that history tracking works.

**`pipeline.sh promote <source-env> <target-env> [--service NAME] [--dry-run]`** — Copy deployment state from a source environment to a target environment. Both environments must exist in the manifest. Services are promoted in dependency order. For each service deployed in the source environment: if the target already has that service at the exact same version, skip it and print `PROMOTE <service> -> <target>: SKIP (same version)`. Otherwise, copy the deployment to the target and print `PROMOTE <service> -> <target> OK`. Services not deployed in the source are silently skipped (not an error). Promote does not require tests to pass — it reuses already-deployed artifacts. With `--service NAME`, only promote that service plus its transitive dependencies. With `--dry-run`, print `DRY-RUN promote from <source> to <target>:` followed by `  Would promote <service>` for each service that would actually change (excluding those that would be skipped). After all services are processed (not in dry-run mode), print a summary line: `Promoted N, skipped M` where N is the number of services actually promoted and M the number skipped. Error if either environment is not in the manifest.

**`pipeline.sh diff <env1> <env2>`** — Compare deployment states of two environments. For every service that appears in either environment's deployment state, output exactly one line, sorted alphabetically by service name:
- `ONLY_IN <env>: <service> (<version>)` — if the service is deployed in one environment but not the other
- `DIFFERS: <service> <env1_version> vs <env2_version> (<envX> newer)` — if the service is deployed in both but at different versions; determine which environment has the newer version by comparing the numeric build timestamp embedded in the version string (format: `<name>:<timestamp>:built`) and append the name of the environment with the higher timestamp
- `SAME: <service> (<version>)` — if the service is deployed in both at the same version

Both environments must exist in the manifest. If an environment has no deployment state yet, treat it as having zero deployed services. Output nothing if neither environment has any deployments.

**`pipeline.sh history <environment> [--service NAME] [--limit N]`** — Display the event log for an environment in reverse chronological order (most recent first). Every successful deploy, promote, and rollback records an event. Each event line follows this format: `<ISO8601_UTC_timestamp> <ACTION> <service> <version>` where ACTION is `DEPLOY`, `PROMOTE`, or `ROLLBACK`, and version is the artifact version string for deploy/promote or `-` for rollback. For `PROMOTE` events specifically, append ` from <source-env>` to indicate where the promotion originated: `<ISO8601_UTC_timestamp> PROMOTE <service> <version> from <source-env>`. Default limit is 10 events. With `--limit N`, show at most N events. With `--service NAME`, filter to only events for that service. The environment must exist in the manifest.

**Modifications to existing commands:** The `deploy` command must now record a `DEPLOY` event for each successfully deployed service. The `rollback` command must record a `ROLLBACK` event for each successfully rolled-back service. The `promote` command records a `PROMOTE` event (including the source environment) for each promoted service (not for skipped ones). All events include an ISO8601 UTC timestamp. The event log is append-only and persists across commands.

rounds.title: Migrate the pipeline orchestrator to semantic versioning. Each service in the manifest mus
rounds.md:
Migrate the pipeline orchestrator to semantic versioning. Each service in the manifest must now include a `version` field in `N.N.N` format (three dot-separated non-negative integers). Add the following initial versions to the manifest:

| Service | Version |
|---------|---------|
| api-gateway | 2.1.0 |
| user-service | 1.3.0 |
| order-service | 1.0.0 |
| notification-service | 0.8.0 |
| analytics-service | 1.9.0 |

This migration changes the build artifact format and version comparison logic across multiple commands.

**Changes to `validate`** — Additionally validate that every service has a `version` field matching the pattern `N.N.N` (three dot-separated non-negative integers). Missing or malformed version is a validation error.

**Changes to `build`** — Build artifact format changes from `<name>:<timestamp>:built` to `<name>:<version>+build.<timestamp>`, where `<version>` is the service's version from the manifest. Example: `api-gateway:2.1.0+build.1713500000`.

**Changes to `diff`** — Version comparison now uses semver-first logic:
1. Extract the semver portion (between the first colon and `+build.`) and the build timestamp (after `+build.`) from each version string.
2. Compare semver components numerically: major, then minor, then patch. Higher semver = newer.
3. If semver is identical, use build timestamp as tiebreaker (higher = newer).
4. If both semver and timestamp are identical, versions are the same.

Comparison must be numeric, not lexicographic: `1.10.0` is newer than `1.9.0`.

The output format remains: `DIFFERS: <service> <v1> vs <v2> (<envX> newer)`.

**Changes to `promote`** — Add downgrade conflict detection and a `--force` flag. When promoting, extract the semver portion from both source and target version strings. If the target environment already has a strictly higher semver for a service, this is a downgrade conflict:

- Without `--force`: print `CONFLICT: <service> -> <target>: downgrade (<source_semver> < <target_semver>)` and do not promote that service.
- With `--force`: override the conflict, promote the service, and print `PROMOTE <service> -> <target> OK (forced)`.
- The summary line changes to: `Promoted N, skipped M, conflicts C` where C is the number of services blocked by conflicts. When `--force` is used, forced overrides count as promotions (not conflicts), so C is zero.
- Conflict-blocked services do NOT generate event log entries. Forced promotions DO generate PROMOTE events.
- In dry-run mode, conflicts show as `  Would conflict <service> (downgrade)` regardless of whether `--force` is specified.

**Changes to `history`** — Forced promotion events include a `(forced)` suffix: `<timestamp> PROMOTE <service> <version> from <source-env> (forced)`.

**New command: `pipeline.sh bump <service> <major|minor|patch>`** — Bump a service's version in the manifest file. The version change is written back to the manifest and affects subsequent `build` commands.

- `major`: increment major version, reset minor and patch to 0 (e.g., `1.3.0` → `2.0.0`)
- `minor`: increment minor version, reset patch to 0 (e.g., `1.3.0` → `1.4.0`)
- `patch`: increment patch version (e.g., `1.3.0` → `1.3.1`)
- Print `BUMP <service> <old_version> -> <new_version>`
- Error if the service does not exist.

rounds.title: The rollback behavior is wrong — rolling back a service should restore the previous versio
rounds.md:
The rollback behavior is wrong — rolling back a service should restore the previous version from event history, not just remove it entirely. Also, the deploy command has been silently overwriting versions without any record of what was there before.

Fix rollback to work as an actual rollback: when you roll back a service from an environment, find the most recent non-rollback event for that service *before* the current deployment and restore that version. If there is no prior version (the service was deployed for the first time), then remove it entirely as before.

The rollback output changes to: `ROLLBACK <service> from <env>: <current_version> -> <previous_version>` (or `ROLLBACK <service> from <env>: <current_version> -> (removed)` if no prior version exists).

Rollback should fail if the service is not currently deployed (same as before).

Additionally, add a `--to-version` flag to rollback that lets you roll back to a specific version from history rather than the most recent previous one. If the specified version doesn't exist in history for that service, print `ERROR: version not found in history for <service>`.

Add a new `pipeline.sh snapshot <environment>` command that captures the complete state of an environment as a named point-in-time snapshot. Each snapshot gets an auto-incrementing ID (starting from 1) and records all services with their current versions and timestamps. Output: `SNAPSHOT <env> #<id> (<N> services)`.

Add `pipeline.sh snapshot --list <environment>` to list all snapshots: `#<id> <iso_timestamp> (<N> services)`.

Add `pipeline.sh restore <environment> <snapshot_id>` to restore an environment to a snapshot state. This replaces the environment's entire service set with the snapshot's recorded versions. Output one line per service: `RESTORE <service> -> <env> (<version>)`, then a summary: `Restored <N> services from snapshot #<id>`. Restore generates RESTORE event log entries for each service.

The history command should now also show RESTORE events: `<timestamp> RESTORE <service> <version> from snapshot #<id>`.

rounds.title: Add environment locking to the pipeline orchestrator. Locking prevents destructive operati
rounds.md:
Add environment locking to the pipeline orchestrator. Locking prevents destructive operations on an environment while allowing read-only queries to continue.

**`pipeline.sh lock <environment> [--reason REASON]`** — Lock an environment. Output: `LOCKED <env>: <reason>` (default reason is `manual lock` if not specified). Locking an already-locked environment updates the reason and prints the same output (idempotent). Error if the environment doesn't exist in the manifest: `ERROR: unknown environment <env>`.

**`pipeline.sh unlock <environment>`** — Unlock an environment. Output: `UNLOCKED <env>`. Unlocking an already-unlocked environment prints the same output (idempotent, not an error). Error if the environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment <env>`.

**`pipeline.sh lock --list`** — List all currently locked environments. Each line: `<env>: <reason> (locked since <ISO8601_timestamp>)`. Print nothing if no environments are locked. Environments are listed alphabetically.

**Lock enforcement** — When an environment is locked, the following commands must fail with `ERROR: <env> is locked (<reason>)` and perform no state changes:
- `deploy` (deploying to a locked environment)
- `promote` (when the target environment is locked; source being locked is OK)
- `rollback` (rolling back from a locked environment)
- `restore` (restoring into a locked environment)

Commands that continue to work on locked environments (read-only): `status`, `diff`, `history`, `snapshot`, `validate`, `order`, `build`, `test`, `graph`, `bump`, `lock`, `unlock`.

**Status integration** — The `status <environment>` detail view (when an environment argument is given) must include lock status. If the environment is locked, add a line at the top before service listings: `[LOCKED: <reason>]`. If unlocked, add nothing extra.

**History integration** — Lock and unlock operations must write events to the environment's event log (the same JSONL file used by deploy, rollback, promote, and restore). This ensures `history` shows a complete audit trail.

- Lock event: `{"timestamp": <unix>, "iso_timestamp": "<ISO8601>", "action": "LOCK", "service": "-", "version": "-", "reason": "<reason>"}`
- Unlock event: `{"timestamp": <unix>, "iso_timestamp": "<ISO8601>", "action": "UNLOCK", "service": "-", "version": "-"}`

The `history <environment>` command must display these events:
- Lock: `<iso_timestamp> LOCK <reason>`
- Unlock: `<iso_timestamp> UNLOCK`

These events appear in chronological order alongside DEPLOY, ROLLBACK, PROMOTE, and RESTORE events.

Lock state must persist across command invocations (use the filesystem).

rounds.title: Add per-environment configuration variable management to the pipeline orchestrator. Config
rounds.md:
Add per-environment configuration variable management to the pipeline orchestrator. Config variables are key-value pairs stored per environment that can be set, queried, listed, and compared.

**`pipeline.sh config set <environment> <KEY> <VALUE>`** — Set a configuration variable for an environment. Keys are case-sensitive strings (alphanumeric and underscores only). Values are arbitrary strings. Output: `CONFIG <env>: <KEY>=<VALUE>`. Setting an existing key updates its value (idempotent). Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`.

**`pipeline.sh config get <environment> <KEY>`** — Get the value of a single config variable. Output: just the value on a single line. Error if key not found: `ERROR: key '<KEY>' not found in <env>`. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`.

**`pipeline.sh config list <environment>`** — List all config variables for an environment, sorted alphabetically by key. Each line: `<KEY>=<VALUE>`. Print nothing if no variables are set. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`.

**`pipeline.sh config diff <env1> <env2>`** — Compare configuration between two environments. Output lines sorted alphabetically by key:
- Keys only in env1: `+ <KEY>=<VALUE> (only in <env1>)`
- Keys only in env2: `- <KEY>=<VALUE> (only in <env2>)`
- Keys in both with different values: `~ <KEY>: <env1_value> -> <env2_value>`
- Keys in both with same values are NOT shown (silent agreement)

If both environments have identical configs (or both empty), print nothing. Error if either environment doesn't exist.

**`pipeline.sh config delete <environment> <KEY>`** — Delete a configuration variable. Output: `CONFIG <env>: deleted <KEY>`. Error if key doesn't exist: `ERROR: key '<KEY>' not found in <env>`. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`.

**`pipeline.sh config export <environment>`** — Export all config variables as a JSON object to stdout. Output is a single line of valid JSON (e.g. `{"DB_HOST":"localhost","DB_PORT":"5432"}`). Keys are sorted alphabetically in the output. If no config variables are set, output `{}`. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`.

**`pipeline.sh config import <environment>`** — Import config variables from JSON provided on stdin. The JSON must be an object with string keys and string values. Each key-value pair is merged into the environment's config (existing keys are updated, new keys are added). Output one `CONFIG <env>: <KEY>=<VALUE>` line per key imported, sorted alphabetically by key. Key validation is applied to every key in the import — if any key is invalid, reject the entire import with `ERROR: invalid key '<KEY>'` (check all keys before writing any). Lock enforcement applies (mutates state). Each imported key generates a CONFIG_SET event in the history.

**Key validation** — Keys passed to `config set`, `config delete`, and `config import` must match the pattern `^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$` (start with letter or underscore, then letters/digits/underscores). Reject invalid keys with: `ERROR: invalid key '<KEY>'`. This validation is applied before any other checks (including lock checks).

**Lock enforcement** — `config set`, `config delete`, and `config import` are blocked on locked environments (they mutate state). The error must match the standard lock format: `ERROR: <env> is locked (<reason>)`. Commands `config get`, `config list`, `config diff`, and `config export` work on locked environments (read-only).

**History integration** — Each `config set` operation writes a CONFIG_SET event, and each `config delete` operation writes a CONFIG_DELETE event to the environment's event log:
- CONFIG_SET event: `{"timestamp": <unix>, "iso_timestamp": "<ISO8601>", "action": "CONFIG_SET", "service": "-", "version": "-", "key": "<KEY>", "value": "<VALUE>"}`
- CONFIG_DELETE event: `{"timestamp": <unix>, "iso_timestamp": "<ISO8601>", "action": "CONFIG_DELETE", "service": "-", "version": "-", "key": "<KEY>"}`
- History display for set: `<iso_timestamp> CONFIG_SET <KEY>=<VALUE>`
- History display for delete: `<iso_timestamp> CONFIG_DELETE <KEY>`

**Status integration** — The `status <environment>` detail view must show config count after lock status (if any) and before service listings: `Config: <N> variables`. Omit this line entirely if no config variables are set for the environment.

Config state is stored in `/app/.pipeline/config/<environment>.json` and persists across command invocations.

rounds.title: Add service replica scaling to the pipeline orchestrator. Replicas represent the number of
rounds.md:
Add service replica scaling to the pipeline orchestrator. Replicas represent the number of instances running for each service in each environment.

**`pipeline.sh scale <environment> <service> <count>`** — Set the replica count for a service in an environment. Count must be a positive integer (>= 1). Output: `SCALED <env>/<service> to <count> replicas (was <previous>)`. Error if count exceeds the environment's `max_replicas` from the manifest: `ERROR: <count> exceeds max_replicas (<max>) for <env>`. Error if count violates service-level bounds (see bounds section): `ERROR: <count> violates bounds [<min>..<max>] for <service> in <env>`. Error if count is not a positive integer: `ERROR: invalid replica count '<count>'`. Error if service doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown service '<service>'`. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`. A service must be deployed to the environment before scaling — error if not deployed: `ERROR: <service> is not deployed to <env>`. Validation order: env exists → service exists → valid integer → deployed → env max_replicas → service bounds.

**`pipeline.sh scale <environment> <service>`** — Get the current replica count for a service. Output: `<service>: <count> replicas`. Default replica count for any deployed service is 1 (if never explicitly scaled). Error if service not deployed: `ERROR: <service> is not deployed to <env>`.

**`pipeline.sh scale <environment>`** — List replica counts for all deployed services in an environment, sorted alphabetically by service name. Each line: `<service>: <count> replicas`. If no services deployed, print nothing. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`.

**`pipeline.sh scale <environment> --all <count>`** — Set the replica count for ALL deployed services in an environment atomically. Before applying, validate that count does not exceed `max_replicas` for the environment AND does not violate any individual service's bounds. If any service's bounds would be violated, fail the entire batch: `ERROR: <count> violates bounds [<min>..<max>] for <service> in <env>` (report the first alphabetically). If valid, scale all deployed services and output one line per service (alphabetically sorted): `SCALED <env>/<service> to <count> replicas (was <previous>)`. Then print a summary line: `Scaled <N> services to <count> replicas`. A SCALE event is written for each service. If no services are deployed, print `ERROR: no services deployed in <env>`. Lock enforcement applies.

**`pipeline.sh scale <environment> <service> --bounds <min> <max>`** — Set autoscaling bounds for a service in an environment. Both min and max must be positive integers. Constraints: min >= 1, max >= min, max <= environment's `max_replicas`. Output: `BOUNDS <env>/<service> set to [<min>..<max>]`. If the current replica count is outside the new bounds, automatically adjust it (scale up to min or down to max) and print an additional line: `SCALED <env>/<service> to <adjusted> replicas (was <previous>)` (plus a SCALE event). Errors: `ERROR: invalid bounds: min must be >= 1`, `ERROR: invalid bounds: max must be >= min`, `ERROR: invalid bounds: max (<max>) exceeds max_replicas (<env_max>) for <env>`. The service must be deployed. Lock enforcement applies. Bounds are stored in `/app/.pipeline/bounds/<environment>.json`.

**`pipeline.sh scale <environment> <service> --bounds`** — Get current bounds for a service. Output: `<service>: bounds [<min>..<max>]`. If no bounds set, output: `<service>: no bounds set`. Service must be deployed.

**Lock enforcement** — `scale <env> <service> <count>`, `scale <env> --all <count>`, and `scale <env> <service> --bounds <min> <max>` (writes) are blocked on locked environments. `scale <env>`, `scale <env> <service>`, and `scale <env> <service> --bounds` (reads) work on locked environments.

**History integration** — Each scale operation writes a SCALE event:
- Event: `{"timestamp": <unix>, "iso_timestamp": "<ISO8601>", "action": "SCALE", "service": "<service>", "version": "-", "replicas": <count>, "previous": <previous_count>}`
- History display: `<iso_timestamp> SCALE <service> from <previous> to <count> replicas`

**Status integration** — The `status <environment>` detail view must show replica count after the version for each service: `<service>: <version> (deployed at <timestamp>) [<count> replicas]`. The `[<count> replicas]` suffix appears on every deployed service line (default 1 if never scaled).

**Deploy interaction** — When a service is deployed (or redeployed), its replica count resets to 1. When a service is rolled back, its replica count resets to 1. When a promote happens, the replica count does NOT transfer — the target service gets replica count 1. Bounds are NOT affected by deploy/rollback/promote — they persist independently.

Replica state is stored in `/app/.pipeline/replicas/<environment>.json` and bounds in `/app/.pipeline/bounds/<environment>.json`. Both persist across command invocations.

rounds.title: Add service health tracking to the pipeline orchestrator. Each deployed service has a heal
rounds.md:
Add service health tracking to the pipeline orchestrator. Each deployed service has a health state that can be healthy, unhealthy, or degraded.

**`pipeline.sh health <environment> <service> set <state>`** — Set the health state for a deployed service. State must be one of: `healthy`, `unhealthy`, `degraded`. Output: `HEALTH <env>/<service> set to <state> (was <previous_state>)`. Default health state for any deployed service is `healthy`. Error if state is invalid: `ERROR: invalid health state '<state>' (must be healthy, unhealthy, or degraded)`. Error if service doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown service '<service>'`. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`. Error if service not deployed: `ERROR: <service> is not deployed to <env>`. Validation order: env exists → service exists → valid state → deployed. Lock enforcement applies (mutation).

**`pipeline.sh health <environment> <service>`** — Get the current health state for a deployed service. Output: `<service>: <state>`. Default is `healthy` if never explicitly set or after deploy/rollback/promote. Error if service not deployed: `ERROR: <service> is not deployed to <env>`. Error if service/env doesn't exist (same as above).

**`pipeline.sh health <environment>`** — List health states for all deployed services in an environment, sorted alphabetically by service name. Each line: `<service>: <state>`. If no services deployed, print nothing. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`.

**`pipeline.sh health <environment> --filter <state>`** — List only services matching the given health state, sorted alphabetically. Each line: `<service>: <state>`. If no services match, print nothing. The filter value must be one of healthy, unhealthy, degraded. Error if invalid: `ERROR: invalid health state '<state>' (must be healthy, unhealthy, or degraded)`. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`.

**Lock enforcement** — `health <env> <service> set <state>` (write) is blocked on locked environments. `health <env> <service>`, `health <env>`, and `health <env> --filter <state>` (reads) work on locked environments.

**History integration** — Each health set operation writes a HEALTH event:
- Event: `{"timestamp": <unix>, "iso_timestamp": "<ISO8601>", "action": "HEALTH", "service": "<service>", "version": "-", "state": "<new_state>", "previous_state": "<old_state>"}`
- History display: `<iso_timestamp> HEALTH <service> <previous_state> -> <new_state>`

**Status integration** — The `status <environment>` detail view must show health state after the replica count for each service: `<service>: <version> (deployed at <timestamp>) [<count> replicas] [<HEALTH_STATE>]`. The health state is displayed in UPPERCASE (e.g., `[HEALTHY]`, `[UNHEALTHY]`, `[DEGRADED]`). Default is `[HEALTHY]` if never set.

**Deploy interaction** — When a service is deployed (or redeployed), its health state resets to `healthy`. When a service is rolled back, its health state resets to `healthy`. When a promote happens, the target service gets health state `healthy` (regardless of source health state). Health state in the source environment is NOT affected by promote.

**Scale interaction** — Cannot scale UP (increase replica count) an unhealthy service. Error: `ERROR: cannot scale up unhealthy service '<service>' in <env>`. Scaling down or to the same count is allowed for unhealthy services. Degraded services CAN be scaled up normally. This restriction also applies to `scale --all`: if any service is unhealthy and the new count would increase its replica count, the entire batch fails with the error for the first alphabetically affected service. The `--all` health pre-check happens after the bounds pre-check.

**Promote interaction** — Cannot promote an unhealthy service from the source environment. If any service in the promotion batch is unhealthy in the source environment, the entire promote fails: `ERROR: cannot promote unhealthy service '<service>' from <source_env>` (report the first alphabetically that is unhealthy). Degraded services CAN be promoted normally. The health check happens before version comparison — even if the version would be skipped, an unhealthy source service still causes an error.

Health state is stored in `/app/.pipeline/health/<environment>.json` as `{"service_name": {"state": "<state>", "state_snapshot": <service_state_dict>}}`. The `state_snapshot` tracks the service's deployment state at the time health was set — when deployment state changes (deploy/rollback/promote), the snapshot no longer matches and the service reverts to default `healthy`.

rounds.title: Add resource quotas to the pipeline orchestrator. Quotas allow setting environment-level l
rounds.md:
Add resource quotas to the pipeline orchestrator. Quotas allow setting environment-level limits on the number of services and total replicas.

**`pipeline.sh quota <environment> set <resource> <limit>`** — Set a resource quota for an environment. Supported resources: `max_services` (maximum number of deployed services) and `max_total_replicas` (maximum sum of all service replica counts). Limit must be a positive integer (>= 1). Output: `QUOTA <env>: <resource>=<limit>`. Error if resource is unsupported: `ERROR: unknown quota resource '<resource>' (must be max_services or max_total_replicas)`. Error if limit is not a positive integer: `ERROR: invalid quota limit '<limit>'`. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<env>'`. Validation order: env exists → valid resource → valid limit. Lock enforcement applies (mutation).

**`pipeline.sh quota <environment> get <resource>`** — Get a specific quota's limit and current usage. Output: `<resource>: <limit> (current: <usage>)`. If quota is not set: `<resource>: not set (current: <usage>)`. Usage for `max_services` is the number of currently deployed services. Usage for `max_total_replicas` is the sum of all service replica counts (default 1 per service if not explicitly scaled). Error if resource is unsupported or environment doesn't exist (same errors as set).

**`pipeline.sh quota <environment> list`** — List all set quotas with current usage. Each line: `<resource>: <limit> (current: <usage>)`. Only shows quotas that have been explicitly set. If no quotas set, print nothing. Error if environment doesn't exist.

**`pipeline.sh quota <environment> remove <resource>`** — Remove a quota. Output: `QUOTA <env>: removed <resource>`. Error if quota was not set: `ERROR: quota '<resource>' not set in <env>`. Error if resource unsupported or env doesn't exist. Lock enforcement applies.

**`pipeline.sh quota <environment> --check`** — Check all quotas and report violations. For each set quota, output: `<resource>: OK (<usage>/<limit>)` if within limit, or `<resource>: EXCEEDED (<usage>/<limit>)` if over. At the end, print `All quotas OK` if none exceeded, or `<N> quota(s) exceeded` if any are over. Error if environment doesn't exist.

**Lock enforcement** — `quota set` and `quota remove` (writes) are blocked on locked environments. `quota get`, `quota list`, and `quota --check` (reads) work on locked environments.

**Deploy enforcement** — When deploying a NEW service (not redeploying an existing one) to an environment, check the `max_services` quota. If deploying this service would cause the number of deployed services to exceed the limit, error: `ERROR: deploying <service> would exceed max_services quota (<current+1>/<limit>) in <env>`. This check happens AFTER the lock check but BEFORE the actual deployment. Re-deploying (updating) an already-deployed service does NOT trigger this check. The check applies per service in the deploy loop — if deploying with dependencies, each new service is checked individually.

**Scale enforcement** — When scaling a service (single `scale set`), check `max_total_replicas`. Calculate the projected total: sum of all current replica counts, minus the service's current count, plus the new count. If projected exceeds the limit, error: `ERROR: scaling <service> to <count> would exceed max_total_replicas quota (<projected>/<limit>) in <env>`. This check happens AFTER the health check but BEFORE the replica update. For `scale --all`, calculate the projected total as: new_count * number_of_services. If this exceeds the limit, error: `ERROR: scaling all to <count> would exceed max_total_replicas quota (<projected>/<limit>) in <env>`. The --all check happens AFTER the health check in the pre-check phase.

**History integration** — Each quota set/remove writes an event:
- Set event: `{"timestamp": <unix>, "iso_timestamp": "<ISO8601>", "action": "QUOTA_SET", "service": "-", "version": "-", "resource": "<resource>", "limit": <limit>}`
- Remove event: `{"timestamp": <unix>, "iso_timestamp": "<ISO8601>", "action": "QUOTA_REMOVE", "service": "-", "version": "-", "resource": "<resource>"}`
- History display: `<iso_timestamp> QUOTA_SET <resource>=<limit>` and `<iso_timestamp> QUOTA_REMOVE <resource>`

**Status integration** — The `status <environment>` overview must show quota info after the config line (if quotas are set). Format: `Quotas: <resource>=<limit> (<usage>/<limit>), ...` — one line with all set quotas, comma-separated. Only shown if at least one quota is set.

Quota state is stored in `/app/.pipeline/quotas/<environment>.json` as `{"resource_name": limit_integer}`.

rounds.title: Add environment cloning to the pipeline orchestrator. Cloning creates a new environment by
rounds.md:
Add environment cloning to the pipeline orchestrator. Cloning creates a new environment by copying the full operational state from an existing source environment.

**`pipeline.sh clone <source_env> <target_env_name> [--max-replicas N]`** — Clone a source environment into a new target environment. The target environment must not already exist (in the manifest or in state). The source environment must exist. The source environment must not be locked. Output on success: `CLONE <source_env> -> <target_env_name> (<N> services)` where N is the number of deployed services copied (0 if source has no deployments).

Error if source environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<source_env>'`. Error if target environment already exists: `ERROR: environment '<target_env_name>' already exists`. Error if source environment is locked: `ERROR: <source_env> is locked (<reason>)`. Validation order: source exists -> target does not exist -> source not locked.

**What is copied** — The clone copies the following state from the source environment to the target:
- Deployed services (deployment state including versions and timestamps)
- Replica counts for each service
- Service-level replica bounds
- Health states (including state snapshots)
- Config variables
- Quotas

**What is NOT copied** — The following are NOT transferred to the target:
- Locks (the new environment starts unlocked)
- Events/history (the new environment starts with a fresh event log containing only the CLONE event)
- Snapshots (the new environment starts with no snapshots)

**Manifest update** — The new environment must be added to the `environments` section of `pipeline.yaml` so it persists across invocations. The new entry uses `deploy_target: "/deployments/<target_env_name>"`, `health_check_port` copied from the source environment, and `max_replicas` set to the value of the `--max-replicas` flag. If `--max-replicas` is not specified, the source environment's `max_replicas` value is used. The `--max-replicas` value must be a positive integer (>= 1); error if invalid: `ERROR: invalid max-replicas '<value>'`. The max-replicas validation happens after the lock check.

**History integration** — A single CLONE event is written to the TARGET environment's event log (the source environment's event log is not modified):
- Event: `{"timestamp": <unix>, "iso_timestamp": "<ISO8601>", "action": "CLONE", "service": "-", "version": "-", "source": "<source_env>"}`
- History display: `<iso_timestamp> CLONE from <source_env>`

**Status integration** — After cloning, `status` (without arguments) must include the new environment in its summary. `status <target_env_name>` must show the cloned services, replicas, health states, config, and quotas exactly as they would appear for any other environment.

**Interaction with other commands** — The cloned environment is fully operational. All existing commands (deploy, rollback, promote, scale, health, config, quota, lock, unlock, snapshot, restore, diff, history) work on the cloned environment the same as any other environment. The cloned environment can itself be used as a source for promote, diff, or further clone operations.

rounds.title: Add an audit report command to the pipeline orchestrator. The audit command generates a co
rounds.md:
Add an audit report command to the pipeline orchestrator. The audit command generates a comprehensive compliance report for an environment, aggregating deployment state, replicas, health, quotas, config, lock status, and recent activity into a single read-only view.

**`pipeline.sh audit <environment>`** — Generate a full audit report for the given environment. The report is entirely read-only: it works on locked environments and does NOT generate any events. Error if the environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<environment>'`.

The report consists of a header, seven sections, and a footer, printed in this exact order:

```
=== Audit Report: <environment> ===
--- Deployment Summary ---
  <service_name>: <version> (deployed <iso_timestamp>)
--- Replica Summary ---
  <service_name>: <count> replicas
--- Health Summary ---
  <service_name>: <state>
--- Quota Status ---
  <resource>: <limit> (current: <usage>) [OK|EXCEEDED]
--- Config Variables ---
  <key_name>
--- Lock Status ---
  Locked: <reason>
--- Recent Activity ---
  <iso_timestamp> <ACTION> <details>
=== End Audit Report ===
```

Each section is described in detail below.

**Deployment Summary** — Lists all deployed services with their version and deployment timestamp. Services are sorted alphabetically by name. Each line is indented with 2 spaces. Format per service: `  <service_name>: <version> (deployed <iso_timestamp>)` where `<version>` is the full artifact version string (e.g., `api-gateway:1713500000:built`) and `<iso_timestamp>` is the ISO 8601 UTC timestamp of the deployment. If no services are deployed, print a single indented line: `  (no services deployed)`.

**Replica Summary** — Shows the replica count for every deployed service, sorted alphabetically by service name. Each line is indented with 2 spaces. Format: `  <service_name>: <count> replicas`. The count is the explicit replica setting if one exists, otherwise 1 (the default for any deployed service). Only deployed services are listed. If no services are deployed, print: `  (no services deployed)`.

**Health Summary** — Shows the health state for every deployed service, sorted alphabetically by service name. Each line is indented with 2 spaces. Format: `  <service_name>: <state>` where `<state>` is one of `healthy`, `unhealthy`, or `degraded`. If a service has no explicit health state set (or its health state has been reset by a deploy/rollback/promote), the default is `healthy`. Only deployed services are listed. If no services are deployed, print: `  (no services deployed)`.

**Quota Status** — Shows all set quotas with their limits, current usage, and compliance status. Quotas are listed in alphabetical order by resource name. Each line is indented with 2 spaces. Format: `  <resource>: <limit> (current: <usage>) [OK]` if usage is within the limit (usage <= limit), or `  <resource>: <limit> (current: <usage>) [EXCEEDED]` if usage exceeds the limit (usage > limit). Usage for `max_services` is the number of currently deployed services. Usage for `max_total_replicas` is the sum of all deployed service replica counts (default 1 per service if not explicitly scaled). If no quotas are set, print: `  (no quotas set)`.

**Config Variables** — Lists the names of all config variables set for the environment, sorted alphabetically. Each line is indented with 2 spaces. Format: `  <key_name>`. Only key names are shown (values are omitted for security). If no config variables are set, print: `  (no config variables)`.

**Lock Status** — Shows whether the environment is currently locked. Indented with 2 spaces. If locked, print: `  Locked: <reason>`. If unlocked, print: `  Unlocked`.

**Recent Activity** — Shows the last 10 events from the environment's event log, ordered newest first (most recent event at the top). Each line is indented with 2 spaces. Format: `  <iso_timestamp> <ACTION> <details>` — the same display format used by the `history` command. If fewer than 10 events exist, show all of them. If no events exist, print: `  (no events)`.

**`pipeline.sh audit <environment> --section <name>`** — Show only the header, the specified section, and the footer. Valid section names: `deployment`, `replicas`, `health`, `quotas`, `config`, `lock`, `activity`. The mapping is: `deployment` shows the Deployment Summary section, `replicas` shows the Replica Summary section, `health` shows the Health Summary section, `quotas` shows the Quota Status section, `config` shows the Config Variables section, `lock` shows the Lock Status section, `activity` shows the Recent Activity section. Error if section name is not one of the valid names: `ERROR: unknown section '<name>' (valid: deployment, replicas, health, quotas, config, lock, activity)`. Error if environment doesn't exist: `ERROR: unknown environment '<environment>'`. Validation order: environment exists -> valid section name.

Output format with `--section`:
```
=== Audit Report: <environment> ===
--- <Section Title> ---
  <section content>
=== End Audit Report ===
```

**`pipeline.sh audit <environment> --json`** — Output the full report as a single line of valid JSON instead of the human-readable format. The JSON structure is:

```json
{
  "environment": "<environment>",
  "deployment": {
    "<service_name>": {"version": "<version>", "timestamp": "<iso_timestamp>"}
  },
  "replicas": {
    "<service_name>": <count>
  },
  "health": {
    "<service_name>": "<state>"
  },
  "quotas": {
    "<resource>": {"limit": <limit>, "current": <usage>, "status": "OK|EXCEEDED"}
  },
  "config": ["<key1>", "<key2>"],
  "lock": {"locked": true, "reason": "<reason>"},
  "activity": [
    {"timestamp": "<iso_timestamp>", "action": "<ACTION>", "details": "<details>"}
  ]
}
```

Details for each JSON field:
- `environment`: the environment name string.
- `deployment`: an object mapping service names to objects with `version` (string) and `timestamp` (ISO 8601 string). Empty object `{}` if no services deployed. Keys sorted alphabetically.
- `replicas`: an object mapping service names to integer counts. Default 1 for deployed services with no explicit replica setting. Empty object `{}` if no services deployed. Keys sorted alphabetically.
- `health`: an object mapping service names to state strings (`healthy`, `unhealthy`, or `degraded`). Default `healthy` if not explicitly set. Empty object `{}` if no services deployed. Keys sorted alphabetically.
- `quotas`: an object mapping resource names to objects with `limit` (integer), `current` (integer), and `status` (`"OK"` or `"EXCEEDED"`). Empty object `{}` if no quotas set. Keys sorted alphabetically.
- `config`: a JSON array of key name strings, sorted alphabetically. Empty array `[]` if no config variables.
- `lock`: an object with `locked` (boolean) and `reason` (string if locked, `null` if unlocked). When unlocked: `{"locked": false, "reason": null}`.
- `activity`: a JSON array of event objects, each with `timestamp` (ISO 8601 string), `action` (string), and `details` (string). Ordered newest first. Limited to last 10 events. Empty array `[]` if no events. The `details` field contains the remaining display text after the action (the same text shown in `history` output after the action word). If there is no additional detail beyond the action, `details` is an empty string.

The `--json` flag cannot be combined with `--section`. If both are provided: `ERROR: --json and --section cannot be combined`. This check happens after environment and section validation.

**Error handling summary** — Validation order for all audit subcommands: (1) environment exists, (2) section name is valid (if `--section` provided), (3) `--json` and `--section` not combined. Error messages:
- Unknown environment: `ERROR: unknown environment '<environment>'`
- Unknown section: `ERROR: unknown section '<name>' (valid: deployment, replicas, health, quotas, config, lock, activity)`
- Combined flags: `ERROR: --json and --section cannot be combined`

**No events** — The audit command is purely read-only. It does NOT write any events to the environment's event log. It does NOT modify any state files.

**Lock interaction** — The audit command works on locked environments without restriction. It never checks or enforces locks since it performs no mutations.
